Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed imperative proverb: negative/quieting imperative followed by an independent declarative clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Silence and patience allow good judgment to arrive; do not speak too quickly in tense moments.
Usage Contexts: family mediation; village discussion; conflict de-escalation; teaching children
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Associated with elder speech in Thiès-area household and courtyard settings where listening before judgment is valued.
First Example Sentence: Ndaaɓaa hoti, a an: «Ɗú ɗekoh, xel hayɗoh.»
Second Example Sentence: Ca ndaje ga, magaa haaloh: «Ɗú ɗekoh, xel hayɗoh.»
Third Example Sentence: A waxtaanu baaga, ɓa dëgël: «Ɗú ɗekoh, xel hayɗoh.»

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Metaphorical equative clause contrasting water and writing; gnomic declarative.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Words or promises without action do not produce lasting results.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on agrarian imagery: water sustains fields, but marks on paper alone cannot feed people.
First Example Sentence: O patron haal na lool, waaye ndox dooɓaa, mbind du yëf.
Second Example Sentence: Ndaaɓi jang, ndox dooɓaa, mbind du yëf.
Third Example Sentence: Ɓa feyɗoh plan gaa, ndox dooɓaa, mbind du yëf.

Idiom (Noon): Kër am ƴaak, oon am ɓaat.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Balanced nominal clause with parallel possessive predicates.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Every household has hidden pressures, and every person carries a burden.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects courtyard-family life in Serer-Noon communities, where private hardship is recognized without public shame.
First Example Sentence: Bul ñaawlu ko; kër am ƴaak, oon am ɓaat.
Second Example Sentence: Mi rangaa ne, kër am ƴaak, oon am ɓaat.
Third Example Sentence: Ndaaɓaa roŋoh: kër am ƴaak, oon am ɓaat.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Identity proverb: topicalized ethnolinguistic noun phrase plus negated existential/declarative clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person grounded in their language and community does not lose their identity.
Usage Contexts: language preservation; education; cultural events; activism
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: References the Cangin linguistic family and Serer-Noon cultural identity around Thiès and surrounding villages.
First Example Sentence: Jàngal xale yi: Caangin suusaa, Noon du réer.
Second Example Sentence: Ca festival gaa, ɓa woy: Caangin suusaa, Noon du réer.
Third Example Sentence: Bu nu fàtte làkk wi, caangin suusaa, Noon du réer.

Idiom (Noon): Kooñ gu ɗif, loxo gu set.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal parallel idiom: body-part metaphor with adjectival predicates.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person may be poor or worn down yet remain honest and dignified.
Usage Contexts: moral teaching; literature; social commentary; praise
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on hand imagery common in manual labor and market exchange, where clean hands symbolize moral integrity.
First Example Sentence: A liggéey ci suuf, kooñ gu ɗif, loxo gu set.
Second Example Sentence: Ñu doyul alal, waaye kooñ gu ɗif, loxo gu set.
Third Example Sentence: Magaa sant ko: kooñ gu ɗif, loxo gu set.

Idiom (Noon): Ɓaat ga ɗaakaa, gëj ga dég.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Personification clause: a concealed voice paired with a listening/receiving community.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Even a quiet complaint or hidden truth eventually becomes known.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: The truth will eventually be heard.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Evokes oral society and communal listening: hidden speech rarely remains hidden forever.
First Example Sentence: Mu neexul wax, waaye ɓaat ga ɗaakaa, gëj ga dég.
Second Example Sentence: Ci kër ga, ɓaat ga ɗaakaa, gëj ga dég.
Third Example Sentence: Njiit li wara xam: ɓaat ga ɗaakaa, gëj ga dég.

Idiom (Noon): Mburu bu dagan, xale du jooy.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional/causal proverb using food imagery and negated result clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: When basic needs are met, distress decreases; care prevents conflict.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Provide what is needed and peace follows.
Usage Contexts: parenting; household management; social welfare; hospitality
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Bread/food imagery connects with hospitality and child care in Senegalese domestic life.
First Example Sentence: Jox leen lekk ji; mburu bu dagan, xale du jooy.
Second Example Sentence: Ci daara, mburu bu dagan, xale du jooy.
Third Example Sentence: Ndey jaa ree: mburu bu dagan, xale du jooy.

Idiom (Noon): Yoon wi ñaaƴ, waaye tank yi xam.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Concessive clause: difficult path contrasted with experienced feet.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Experienced people can manage hardship because they know the road.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses path-and-feet imagery from walking between neighborhoods, fields, markets, and villages around Thiès.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Noon): /joːn wi ɲaːƴ waaje taŋk ji xam/
First Example Sentence: Bul ragal; yoon wi ñaaƴ, waaye tank yi xam.
Second Example Sentence: Sëriñ bi indi nu, yoon wi ñaaƴ, waaye tank yi xam.
Third Example Sentence: A dajal liggéeykat yi: yoon wi ñaaƴ, waaye tank yi xam.

Idiom (Noon): Saaga du togg ci ndab bu saf.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative metaphorical clause: insulting speech compared to cooking in a tasty pot.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Harsh words cannot create a good relationship or productive outcome.
Usage Contexts: conflict; workplace feedback; family argument; public debate
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Based on cooking metaphors: good food requires balance, just as good speech requires restraint.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Noon): /saːga du togg ci ndab bu saf/
First Example Sentence: Bul ko xas; saaga du togg ci ndab bu saf.
Second Example Sentence: Ca biro ba, ɓa fàttali ko: saaga du togg ci ndab bu saf.
Third Example Sentence: Wax ko ak teey, ndax saaga du togg ci ndab bu saf.

Idiom (Noon): Ƴaal ga ŋgërëm, ndaje ga wér.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Causal communal proverb: praise/gratitude noun phrase followed by healthy-meeting clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Respect and gratitude keep community relations healthy.
Usage Contexts: community meetings; ceremonies; workplace teamwork; family life
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in greeting, thanks, and public acknowledgment practices at gatherings and rites of passage.
First Example Sentence: Nu sant ɓeen ñépp; ƴaal ga ŋgërëm, ndaje ga wér.
Second Example Sentence: Magaa tàmbali ak ƴaal ga ŋgërëm, ndaje ga wér.
Third Example Sentence: Ci ngente ga, ɓa wax: ƴaal ga ŋgërëm, ndaje ga wér.
